title: AirDrop and Quick Share Flaws Let Nearby Attackers Trigger Crashes and Bypass Checks
blurb: Researchers at the CISPA Helmholtz Center disclosed six security flaws in Apple's AirDrop and Google's Quick Share that let an attacker within wireless range crash the file-sharing services or bypass session checks, with no prior connection or user prompt. Apple and Google have begun shipping patches and assigning CVEs, though some bugs remain in coordinated disclosure and no real-world exploitation has been reported.

title: Chinese Z.ai's latest model tops AI ranking charts amid Anthropic Fable 5 ban — blacklisted China firm's popular open-weight GLM-5.2 AI model powered by Huawei silicon
blurb: Z.ai's open-weight GLM-5.2, released under an MIT license and trained on roughly 100,000 Huawei Ascend chips with no Nvidia hardware, climbed to the top of the openly available leaderboards within a week of launch. The model leads Artificial Analysis's Intelligence Index and Design Arena's coding board, a position helped by a US export directive that forced Anthropic to disable Fable 5 worldwide, even as it still trails that Anthropic pair on longer-horizon benchmarks.

title: Attackers Exploit SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558 to Deploy TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer
blurb: Attackers are exploiting a maximum-severity SimpleHelp auth bypass (CVE-2026-48558, CVSS 10.0) to deploy TaskWeaver loader and cross-platform Djinn Stealer.

title: China’s Meituan says its new AI model was trained on domestic chips
blurb: Meituan released LongCat-2.0, a 1.6-trillion-parameter open-weight model it says was both pre-trained and served entirely on a 50,000-chip domestic compute cluster, the first model of its scale to skip Nvidia end-to-end. The company claims performance comparable to Google's Gemini 3.1 Pro, making the model a test of whether China can build frontier-scale AI without US silicon despite Washington's export controls.

title: Supreme Court Rules Constitutional Privacy Protections Apply to Cellphone Users’ Location History
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that constitutional privacy protections cover cellphone location history, holding that users don't forfeit Fourth Amendment expectations just by opting into Google's location tracking. Writing for the majority, Justice Kagan rejected the third-party doctrine for geofence warrants, though the court declined to decide whether the specific search was constitutional and sent the case back to a lower court.

title: Meta fights soaring hardware costs by reusing old DDR4 server memory in new DDR5-only servers — custom CXL 2.0 chip marries legacy DDR4-2400 with cutting-edge DDR5-6400
blurb: Meta built a custom CXL 2.0 memory-expander ASIC called Vistara to bolt DDR4 modules salvaged from decommissioned servers onto new DDR5-only AMD EPYC Turin systems, sidestepping record-high DDR5 prices. Each MemServer pairs 768GB of local DDR5-6400 with 256GB of CXL-attached DDR4-2400 exposed as a separate NUMA tier, letting Linux park cold pages on the slower memory while keeping hot data in fast local DRAM.

title: Nasdaq expands distribution of its market data into blockchain infrastructure
blurb: Nasdaq will distribute its TotalView full depth-of-book equity data through the Pyth Network, putting one of its core market data products on blockchain infrastructure. Publishing on the Pyth Data Marketplace lets developers and institutional users access Nasdaq data via a programmable interface rather than traditional terminals, joining contributors like Tradeweb, SGX, and the U.S. Department of Commerce as Wall Street builds for tokenized assets.

title: Nvidia reportedly cancels quad-die Rubin Ultra GPU in favor of dual-GPU design, report claims — complex design purportedly scrapped over 'manufacturing execution concerns'
blurb: Nvidia has reportedly canceled the four-chiplet version of its Rubin Ultra AI accelerator, switching to an easier-to-build dual-GPU design over manufacturing execution concerns, according to SemiAnalysis. The change would roughly halve the chip's peak performance and cut its HBM4E memory from 16 modules to eight, potentially softening demand in the broader HBM market while making Rubin Ultra cheaper but less competitive against AMD's Instinct MI500 series.

title: The Realities of AI Video Surveillance
blurb: A Financial Times report details how AI now lets surveillance operators run natural-language searches across video feeds, asking for behaviors like two people exchanging a bag or a vehicle that keeps passing the same spot. The shift moves surveillance from a few dozen preset object searches to near-unlimited behavioral queries, which Schneier frames as AI enabling mass spying the way networks once enabled mass surveillance.

title: Meituan launches LongCat-2.0 1.6T parameter model on APIs
blurb: Meituan released LongCat-2.0, a 1.6 trillion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model with roughly 48B active parameters per token, a 1M-token context window, and OpenAI- and Anthropic-compatible APIs aimed at agentic coding and tool use. The model is API-only for now at $0.75 per 1M input tokens and $2.95 per 1M output, with downloadable weights still pending under an MIT-licensed public repo.

title: Meta releases version two of its brain-computer interface that can turn thoughts into keypresses — non-invasive magnetoencephalography scanner can measure changes in brain activity
blurb: Meta released the second version of Brain2Qwerty, a non-invasive brain-computer interface that uses a magnetoencephalography scanner to translate thoughts into keystrokes without surgery. Trained on ten times more data per subject, average word accuracy rose to 61% from 40%, but Meta says that remains too low for clinical use and the MEG hardware is still larger than the user and their chair.

title: JPMorgan broadens blockchain settlement network as banks modernize cross-border payments
blurb: JPMorgan added five Asia-Pacific currencies to its Kinexys blockchain platform, letting institutional clients settle payments and FX in eight currencies around the clock. The bank says Kinexys has processed over $4 trillion to date, and early adopters including Payoneer and JERA Global Markets are using the new Australian dollar and Japanese yen accounts to move funds across borders outside local banking hours.

title: European digital ID wallets rely on safety services of Google and Apple
blurb: European digital identity wallets being rolled out across the EU depend on Google's Play Integrity API and Apple's Managed Device Attestation to verify that wallet apps run on untampered hardware. The article argues this embeds private gatekeepers into public infrastructure, since Google's API excludes non-licensed Android builds and pushes Play Store installs, reinforcing the monopoly the EU claims to oppose while an open Hardware Attestation alternative goes unused.

title: Microsoft adds smarter bot protection to Teams meetings
blurb: Microsoft Teams admins can now require approval before third-party bots join meetings, blocking unapproved note-takers and malicious apps.

title: N.I.H. Announces World’s Largest Integrated Health Database
blurb: The NIH launched what it calls the world's largest integrated health database, pairing more than 500,000 people's full genomes with their clinical records and wearable-device data. The combined dataset lets researchers cross-reference genetic, medical, and real-time activity signals in one place, expanding the statistical power to study how diseases develop and how treatments perform across diverse populations.

title: Taiwan raids Supermicro and two supply-chain partners in widening Nvidia smuggling probe — nine sites hit as six people summoned for questioning
blurb: Taiwan's Keelung District Prosecutors' Office raided Supermicro's Taiwan office, six homes, and three affiliated sites on Monday, widening its first criminal probe into the diversion of Nvidia AI chips to China. Investigators also searched distributor Albatron Technology and data center operator Chief Telecom, but because Taiwanese law doesn't criminalize unauthorized AI chip exports, prosecutors are charging document forgery rather than the smuggling itself. Supermicro shares fell 8%.

title: Hacking a Reverse Osmosis Water Filter Through its Smart Faucet
blurb: Reverse-engineering the serial protocol on a Waterdrop RO filter's smart faucet to wire it into Home Assistant via ESPHome.

title: White House AI crackdown opens door for Chinese model makers to close gap
blurb: The White House let Anthropic resume selling its Mythos 5 model to select companies and federal agencies after a two-week export-control shutdown, while Fable 5 stays off the market and OpenAI agreed to limit its GPT 5.6 rollout. The restrictions arrive as Chinese labs close in, with Zhipu's GLM 5.2 matching top US models on some cyber benchmarks at roughly a quarter of the cost per token.

title: Exclusive: Meta and OpenAI alumni seek $400m for new AI lab
blurb: Three Meta and OpenAI alumni are raising $400M for Aire, a stealth-mode AI lab still in fundraising, according to three sources cited by Sifted. The startup will operate across the US, France, and Israel, joining a wave of well-pedigreed founders chasing nine-figure seed rounds to compete with their former employers.

title: AI browsers like Perplexity Comet can be tricked into spilling your password through BioShocking exploit
blurb: Security researchers at LayerX disclosed BioShocking, an attack that reframes credential theft as a puzzle game to talk AI browsers past their own safety rules, exposing saved passwords, session cookies, and tokens. All six agents tested fell for it, including ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity Comet, and Anthropic's Claude extension, though OpenAI has patched Atlas while Perplexity closed the report without acting.

title: Three AirDrop vulnerabilities discovered, with Apple working on a full fix
blurb: Security researchers disclosed three AirDrop vulnerabilities affecting iPhone and Mac, each letting a nearby attacker crash AirDrop, AirPlay, Handoff, Universal Clipboard, and Continuity Camera with a single malformed request and hold them offline by looping it. No data is exposed, but the proximity attack needs no pairing or shared network, and Apple has patched one bug while still working on the other two.

title: SAP Gives AI Product Oversight to Top Executives in Reshuffle
blurb: SAP distributed oversight of its AI product and engineering functions to top executives including the CEO and COO in its second major reorganization this year. The reshuffle reflects pressure on Europe's largest software company to keep pace with rising AI competition, signaling that SAP views AI product direction as a board-level priority rather than a delegated engineering concern.

title: Meta is telling engineers to handle Claude Code and Codex with care
blurb: Meta has placed strict limits on how engineers in its applied AI division use Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex, The Information reported, over fears that the rival tools' output could inadvertently distill into Meta's own training data. One internal memo told some teams to pause tasks using the outside products, warning of "serious escalations with partner companies" as Meta builds MetaCode to replace them.

title: Apple may have to allow 3rd-party app payments and Apple Pay rivals in UK
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority proposed forcing Apple to let developers steer iPhone users to third-party payment options outside the App Store, and to open the NFC chip to Apple Pay rivals. The CMA preempts the "malicious compliance" tactics Apple used in the EU and US by requiring that any steering fees be fair, reasonable, and lower than current commissions; the proposal is open for comment.

title: How Spain's LaLiga uses nationwide IP blocking to combat illegal sports streams, impacting an estimated 550K+ domains, putting it in conflict with Cloudflare (Bloomberg)
blurb: Spain's LaLiga has been using nationwide IP blocking to shut down illegal sports streams, a tactic Bloomberg reports has swept up an estimated 550K+ domains and pitted the league against Cloudflare. Because the blocks target shared IP addresses rather than individual sites, legitimate small businesses and government agencies are getting knocked offline whenever they happen to sit behind the same infrastructure as a pirate stream.

title: How the Supreme Court Ushered in Corporate Chaos in D.C.
blurb: The Supreme Court affirmed the Federal Reserve's political independence while expanding presidential control over dozens of other federal regulators. The author argues this split ruling leaves agencies like the FTC and SEC newly exposed to White House influence, injecting uncertainty into the rules that govern corporate America.

title: This motor could be the future of e-bikes
blurb: Avinox and Gobao unveiled motor gearbox units at Eurobike that fold an electric motor and an electronic continuously variable transmission into one housing, eliminating the derailleur and cassette. Shipping in 2027, the eCVT lets riders pick a pedaling cadence and have gearing adjust automatically, bringing high-end e-mountain-bike tech toward standard e-bikes.

title: An Indonesian court sentences Gojek co-founder and ex-education minister Nadiem Makarim to 10 years in prison for power abuses over a Chromebook contract (New York Times)
blurb: An Indonesian court sentenced Nadiem Makarim, Gojek co-founder and former education minister, to 10 years in prison for abusing his power over a government Chromebook procurement contract. The case has stoked concerns about judicial fairness in Indonesia, where foreign investors were already turning cautious about the country's legal and political risk.

title: Spain's War on Football Piracy Knocks Legitimate Sites Offline
blurb: LaLiga's anti-piracy system blocks IP addresses to shut down illegal football streams, but the broad blocks are knocking offline legitimate sites that share infrastructure with the pirates, including small businesses and government agencies. The collateral damage shows how blunt IP-level enforcement breaks when targets sit behind shared hosting and CDNs, turning a copyright fight into widespread internet outages across Spain.

title: Lee’s $880 Billion AI Bet Ties Legacy to South Korea Chip Boom
blurb: South Korean President Lee Jae Myung is staking his legacy on an $880B plan to turn the country's underdeveloped southwest into a global semiconductor hub. The bet builds on South Korea's position as a leading beneficiary of the AI boom, anchored by chipmakers Samsung and SK Hynix, and aims to spread that growth beyond the existing manufacturing clusters near Seoul.

title: Orbital files plans for 100,000 orbital data centers
blurb: Orbital, a five-month-old startup, asked the FCC for permission to deploy up to 100,000 data center satellites delivering 10 gigawatts of computing power in low Earth orbit to meet AI demand. The June 24 filing details 100-kilowatt-class spacecraft at 500-850 kilometers, with optical links to constellations like Starlink, joining Starcloud, SpaceX, and Blue Origin in pitching orbital compute as terrestrial data centers hit power and land limits.

title: Sensitive iPhone supplier details were part of last week's data leak at Tata Electronics
blurb: A cyberattack on Tata Electronics exposed over 630GB of data, including sensitive supplier and component details for Apple's unreleased iPhone 18 Pro. The leaked documents, posted to the dark web, cover hundreds of parts spanning circuit-board chips, battery, and camera elements, and could weaken Apple's hand in future supplier negotiations as it confronts rising costs from RAM shortages.

title: Call-centre stocks slide as investors worry AI makes them uninvestable
blurb: Concentrix cut its 2026 revenue forecast and missed on margins, sending its shares down more than 21% and dragging Paris-listed rival Teleperformance down around 13%. The miss hardened a two-year fear into a thesis that conversational AI will absorb the human voice and text support that is the call-centre industry's core product, leaving analysts to label the sector uninvestable.

title: Panasonic to localise US data-centre battery production, CEO says
blurb: Panasonic will mass-produce data-center battery cells at a Kansas plant by fiscal 2028, building them domestically rather than importing to supply US data-center operators directly. The company is directing about 350 billion yen ($2.18B) of a 500 billion yen AI infrastructure budget to its Energy unit, which also supplies Tesla, betting that AI compute growth will pull grid-scale storage demand up with it.

title: Apple and Google sat for discussions to unlock 50W wireless charging for smartphones
blurb: Apple, Google, Xiaomi, and more than 20 companies met in Beijing under the Wireless Power Consortium to finalize the Qi 50W wireless charging standard, testing prototype hardware and cross-brand interoperability. The spec is expected to be finalized in 2028, leaving the open standard trailing proprietary fast-charging tech that smartphone makers like OnePlus have already pushed past 50W.

title: European deeptech investment hits annual record in just six months
blurb: European deeptech startups raised enough capital in the first half of 2026 to break the all-time annual fundraising record, according to Sifted data. The pace suggests investors are concentrating bets on defense, energy, and frontier hardware despite a tighter overall venture market, with the full-year total now on track to far exceed any prior year.

title: China’s Meituan says its new AI model was trained on domestic chips
blurb: Meituan released LongCat-2.0, a 1.6-trillion-parameter open-source model the company says is the first of its scale trained and served end-to-end on a 50,000-chip domestic compute cluster. The claim targets US export controls by arguing China can build frontier-scale models without Nvidia, and Meituan says performance is comparable to Google's Gemini 3.1 Pro.

title: Dragonfly v2.5.0 is released
blurb: Dragonfly v2.5.0 adds direct model repository downloads from Hugging Face and ModelScope via P2P, Kubernetes webhook injection, and comprehensive rate limiting.

title: Chinese Z.ai's latest model tops AI ranking charts amid Anthropic Fable 5 ban — blacklisted China firm's popular open-weight GLM-5.2 AI model powered by Huawei silicon
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an open-weight model under a permissive MIT license that it says was trained entirely on roughly 100,000 Huawei Ascend chips with no Nvidia hardware. Within a week it topped the openly available leaderboards, scoring 51 on Artificial Analysis's Intelligence Index v4.1 and 62.1 on SWE-bench Pro, aided by a U.S. export directive that forced Anthropic to disable Fable 5 worldwide.

title: Nvidia reportedly cancels quad-die Rubin Ultra GPU in favor of dual-GPU design, report claims — complex design purportedly scrapped over 'manufacturing execution concerns'
blurb: Nvidia reportedly canceled the quad-die version of its Rubin Ultra AI accelerator, due in 2027, in favor of an easier-to-manufacture dual-GPU design, according to a SemiAnalysis report Nvidia has not confirmed. The four-chiplet design was scrapped over manufacturing execution concerns tied to packaging four reticle-sized dies and cooling 16 HBM4E modules, leaving the revised part roughly half as powerful and reliant on eight HBM4E modules instead.

title: Decades-Old Bash Tricks Expose AI Coding Agents to Supply Chain Attacks
blurb: Adversa AI disclosed GuardFall, a structural flaw that lets decades-old Bash tricks like quote removal and $IFS spacing slip malicious commands past the shell guards in open-source AI coding agents. Across 11 popular agents including Hermes, OpenCode, and Roo-code, 10 left the gap open, exposing developers to credential theft and wiped environments when agents run with full account authority in auto-yes CI pipelines.

title: Meituan launches LongCat-2.0 1.6T parameter model on APIs
blurb: Meituan released LongCat-2.0, a 1.6T-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model with roughly 48B active parameters per token, available now through OpenAI- and Anthropic-compatible APIs at $0.75 per 1M input and $2.95 per 1M output tokens. The model offers a 1M-token context window, supports Claude Code and Codex-style workflows, and is the system behind the Owl Alpha model previously running on OpenRouter, though downloadable weights remain pending.

title: The Realities of AI Video Surveillance
blurb: The Financial Times reports that AI is shifting video surveillance from preset object searches to open-ended natural language queries, letting analysts ask questions like "two men handing a bag to each other" across massive video streams. Schneier argues this marks the arrival of mass spying, with intelligence services in Israel, Iran, and Russia now able to hunt for behavior rather than objects.

title: Taiwan raids Supermicro and two supply-chain partners in widening Nvidia smuggling probe — nine sites hit as six people summoned for questioning
blurb: Taiwan's Keelung District prosecutors raided Supermicro's Taiwan office, six homes, and affiliated sites including distributor Albatron Technology and data center operator Chief Telecom, expanding the island's first criminal probe into Nvidia AI chip diversion to China. Because Taiwanese law doesn't classify unauthorized AI chip exports as a crime, prosecutors are leaning on forgery and fraud charges, while Taipei weighs new legislation that would let it prosecute smuggling as an export crime for the first time.

title: White House AI crackdown opens door for Chinese model makers to close gap
blurb: The Trump administration's export-control crackdown forced Anthropic into a two-week shutdown and still keeps its Fable 5 model off the market, while OpenAI agreed to limit its GPT 5.6 rollout after a government request. Researchers say Zhipu's GLM 5.2, released this month, now matches top U.S. labs on some cyber benchmarks at roughly a quarter of the cost per token.

title: Microsoft unveils Memora to tackle AI agents’ memory problem
blurb: Microsoft Research introduced Memora, a memory system for AI agents that decouples what is stored from how it is retrieved, organizing knowledge around abstractions and cue-based lookup instead of raw conversation history. Microsoft claims the approach cuts context token usage by up to 98% while matching or exceeding full-context accuracy on long-horizon recall tasks.

title: AI browsers like Perplexity Comet can be tricked into spilling your password through BioShocking exploit
blurb: Security researchers at LayerX disclosed BioShocking, a prompt-injection attack that reframes credential theft as a game objective to bypass AI browser guardrails and exfiltrate saved passwords, session cookies, and tokens. All six browsers tested handed over real credentials, including ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity Comet, and Anthropic's Claude extension, though OpenAI patched Atlas while Perplexity closed the report without acting.

title: Exclusive: Meta and OpenAI alumni seek $400m for new AI lab
blurb: A trio of Meta and OpenAI alumni are seeking $400M for Aire, a stealth-mode AI startup, according to three sources with direct knowledge of the raise. The company plans to operate across the US, France, and Israel, positioning it as one of the larger early-stage European-linked AI labs currently raising.

title: BOE’s Breeden Says AI Agents Could Amplify Market Stress
blurb: Bank of England Deputy Governor Sarah Breeden said autonomous AI agents could amplify volatility during periods of market stress and may require tighter regulation. Speaking at the European Central Bank's annual symposium in Sintra, Portugal, she pointed to the systemic risk of agents reacting in correlated ways across financial markets.

title: MongoDB embeds reranking into Atlas as enterprises look to simplify AI stacks for scale
blurb: MongoDB introduced Native Reranking for Atlas, a public-preview feature powered by Voyage AI that runs inside the aggregation pipeline and can improve retrieval quality by up to 30%. Embedding reranking in the database removes a separate service, API, and orchestration layer, cutting the retry logic, failure handling, and governance overhead that a standalone reranker adds as AI applications scale.

title: Zilliz has a Loonatic storage engine
blurb: Zilliz released Loon, a storage engine that serves real-time vector search, large-scale discovery, and batch analytics from a single copy of data on low-cost object storage. Loon stores scalar fields in Parquet and vectors in Vortex under a shared row ID space and versioned manifest, letting teams add a new embedding model as a column instead of rewriting existing data and cutting data pulled per record read by about 135x versus Parquet.

title: Meta is telling engineers to handle Claude Code and Codex with care
blurb: Meta has placed strict limits on how engineers in its applied AI division use Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex, fearing that the rival tools' output could inadvertently seep into the training data for MetaCode, its in-house replacement. One internal memo told some teams to pause tasks using the outside tools, warning that accidental distillation could breach the rivals' terms of service and trigger lawsuits.

title: Featuring Every Eval Ever Results on Hugging Face Model Pages
blurb: Hugging Face and the EvalEval Coalition launched EEE, a single JSON schema that records who ran an evaluation, which model, how it was accessed, generation settings, and what each metric means, so harness logs, leaderboard scrapes, and paper numbers share one format. The datastore has grown to roughly 229,000 results across 22,000 models and 2,200 benchmarks, and a new converter writes them into Hugging Face Community Evals on model pages.

title: Vorlon debuts Guardian to block risky AI agent actions before they complete
blurb: Vorlon launched Guardian, a real-time enforcement gateway that sits at the protocol layer between AI agents and connected systems to block policy-violating actions before a transaction completes rather than flagging them afterward. Guardian can mask sensitive data in transit, downgrade agents to read-only access, and covers any app or data store with an API or MCP server, including legacy and AI-built tools.

title: GV’s Dave Munichiello On Qualcomm’s Modular Purchase, The Firm’s 10x Return And The Shift In AI Software
blurb: Qualcomm acquired Modular, a Palo Alto startup whose software lets developers run AI models across different chip types, days before chip startup SambaNova was reported to be closing an $800M round at a $10B valuation. GV partner Dave Munichiello, who led early investments in both, argues that as hardware stays scarce and expensive, the software layers connecting chips are becoming as valuable as the silicon itself.

title: Bloome launches chat platform for AI agent teams
blurb: Bloome launched a chat platform where people and multiple AI agents work in one shared conversation, with agents drafting, challenging, and refining each other's output.

title: Ornith-1.0: Self-scaffolding LLMs for agentic coding
blurb: Deep-Reinforce released Ornith-1.0, an open-source family of agentic coding models spanning 9B Dense to 397B MoE, built on Gemma 4 and Qwen 3.5. Its self-improving framework jointly learns solution rollouts and the task-specific harnesses that guide them, with the 397B variant scoring 77.5 on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and 82.4 on SWE-Bench Verified to edge out Claude Opus 4.7.

title: Startup OpenMatter wants to make enterprises prove what their AI agents do
blurb: OpenMatter launched a platform that uses cryptography to give enterprises provable records of how their AI agents act across systems they don't control.

title: An Indonesian court sentences Gojek co-founder and ex-education minister Nadiem Makarim to 10 years in prison for power abuses over a Chromebook contract (New York Times)
blurb: An Indonesian court sentenced Gojek co-founder and former education minister Nadiem Makarim to 10 years in prison for abusing his power over a Chromebook procurement contract. The case has stoked worries about judicial fairness in a market where foreign investors were already pulling back. For founders building in emerging markets, it is a reminder that the line between government service and entrepreneurship can carry personal legal risk when political winds shift.

title: How the Supreme Court Ushered in Corporate Chaos in D.C.
blurb: The Supreme Court left the Federal Reserve's political independence intact but handed the presidency direct control over dozens of other federal regulators, from the FTC to the agencies that police competition, labor, and consumer protection. The practical effect is that rule-making and enforcement now swing with each administration, so the regulatory baseline a company plans around can shift the moment power changes hands. Founders in regulated markets should treat compliance assumptions as variable, not fixed, and build for a rulebook that can be rewritten faster than any business cycle.

title: Exclusive: Meta and OpenAI alumni seek $400m for new AI lab
blurb: Three Meta and OpenAI alumni are raising $400m for Aire, a stealth AI lab split across the US, France, and Israel. The round stands out because it is a seed-stage bet on pedigree, not product, at a size that used to take a Series B. Founder credentials are now the scarcest input in frontier AI, and the labs with the deepest pockets keep paying up for the people who have already shipped at OpenAI and Meta.

title: White House AI crackdown opens door for Chinese model makers to close gap
blurb: Washington's export-control crackdown on US frontier labs is handing China room to catch up. Anthropic spent two weeks dark before the White House cleared a limited Mythos 5 release, Fable 5 stays off the market, and OpenAI agreed to throttle its GPT 5.6 rollout, all while Zhipu shipped GLM 5.2, which matches top US labs on some cyber benchmarks at roughly a quarter the cost per token. The constraint that was supposed to protect the US lead is now the thing slowing it, and the corporate buyers footing the bill have a credible, cheaper Chinese option.

title: GV’s Dave Munichiello On Qualcomm’s Modular Purchase, The Firm’s 10x Return And The Shift In AI Software
blurb: The value in the AI stack is migrating off the silicon and onto the software that abstracts it. Qualcomm just acquired Modular, a startup that lets developers run AI models across different chip types, while SambaNova is closing an $800M round at a $10B valuation led by General Atlantic. As hardware stays scarce and inference goes heterogeneous across Nvidia, AMD, and custom silicon, the layer that makes any chip usable is becoming as valuable as the chip itself, and GV's Dave Munichiello, who backed both companies early, expects that shift to keep pulling consolidation toward the software middle.

title: Lee’s $880 Billion AI Bet Ties Legacy to South Korea Chip Boom
blurb: South Korean President Lee Jae Myung is staking his legacy on an $880B plan to turn the country's underdeveloped southwest into a global chip manufacturing hub. South Korea is already one of the biggest winners of the AI boom, with Samsung and SK Hynix supplying the memory that AI compute runs on. The bet ties a national political agenda to a single industrial cycle, which pays off enormously if AI demand holds and leaves the region exposed if the buildout outruns it.

title: 10x Coding Speed Won't 10x Revenue
blurb: Faster code doesn't translate into faster revenue, because product success has always been about the market, not engineering speed. If AI lets you build 100 products where you once built 10, the win count stays flat at around 4, dropping the success ratio from 4-in-10 to 4-in-100. Software budgets aren't growing 10x and competitors are using the same tools, so the real bottleneck moves to go-to-market.

title: The Agent Playbooks from Salesforce, Snowflake, Databricks, Harvey, and Lovable: Inside SaaStr AI 2026
blurb: The main stage at SaaStr AI 2026 ran end to end on agents in production, carrying quota, writing to systems of record, and reshaping how companies get built. The pattern across Salesforce, Snowflake, Databricks, Harvey, and Lovable was the same: ship agentic features customers will actually pay for, or watch the CPO job go from the cushiest role in B2B to the most stressful one in 18 months. Webflow's number makes the urgency concrete, with answer engines now driving about 50% of site visitors, up from 10% a year ago, so a brand absent from them is invisible to buyers.

title: Google’s VC arm backs ex-Axiom Space CEO’s $30M bet to build a stock exchange for space
blurb: GV co-led a $30M seed round into Nebex, a startup building capital-markets infrastructure for the space economy, founded by ex-Axiom Space CEO Tejpaul Bhatia. The pitch is that ambitious space founders keep struggling to fund complex sovereign programs because the financing rails that exist in every other industry never got built for orbit. With a space economy worth somewhere between $470B and $626B in 2026 and expected to roughly double by the mid-2030s, the bet is that legacy contractor financing can't serve the larger, more international pool now competing for contracts.

title: Call Center Stocks Fall on Worry AI Makes Them ‘Uninvestible’
blurb: Two call-center stocks sold off again after fresh signs that companies are routing customer service to AI instead of human agents. Investors are no longer pricing this as a margin headwind but as an existential one, with some labeling the sector "uninvestible." The shift matters beyond call centers: it is the first public market to treat AI substitution as a reason to abandon a business model entirely, not just discount it. Any founder whose value rests on labor arbitrage should expect that re-rating to come for their category next.

title: Panasonic to localise US data-centre battery production, CEO says
blurb: Panasonic plans to mass-produce data-centre battery cells at a plant in Kansas by fiscal 2028, building them onshore rather than importing. The company is redirecting about 350 billion yen, roughly $2.18B, of a 500 billion yen AI infrastructure budget to its Energy unit, the same division that supplies Tesla, and calls its 950 billion yen data-centre storage sales target a "minimum commitment." The battery business that grew up around electric vehicles is being retooled for the server hall, because AI compute draws spiky loads that demand large-scale storage to bridge outages and smooth supply.

title: Call-centre stocks slide as investors worry AI makes them uninvestable
blurb: Concentrix cut its full-year 2026 revenue outlook to about $10B and its shares fell more than 21%, dragging Paris-listed leader Teleperformance down roughly 13% on no news of its own. The trim was modest, but it hardened a two-year fear into a number, with analysts now circulating the word "uninvestable" for the call-centre sector. The two largest operators sell exactly what conversational AI is built to absorb, so every advance in automated agents reads to the market as a direct subtraction from their addressable business.

title: Exclusive: No More Side Hustles: Why AI Startup Omnea Will Give Employees $250K To Openly Plan Their Next Startup
blurb: Omnea is turning employee entrepreneurial ambition into a recruiting and retention asset rather than a threat, launching a fund that gives staff with five years of service a shot at $250K in seed funding to start their own companies. Eligible employees pitch once for 30 minutes to the CEO and partner fund Firedrop, get a decision in 24 hours, and can take a $10M-cap baseline worth 2.5% or an uncapped SAFE. The bet is that surfacing the side-hustle taboo openly keeps your best people longer and gives you equity in what they build next.

title: Meta is telling engineers to handle Claude Code and Codex with care
blurb: Meta has placed strict limits on how engineers in its applied AI division use Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex, per The Information, fearing that the rivals' output could leak into training data for its own MetaCode tool and constitute inadvertent distillation. The restrictions, which paused some tasks outright, underscore both Anthropic's growing leverage over the firms that depend on it and the industry-wide push to cut spending on outside coding tools.

title: Ornith-1.0: Self-scaffolding LLMs for agentic coding
blurb: Deep-Reinforce released Ornith-1.0, an open-source family of agentic coding models (9B to 397B MoE) trained with a self-improving framework where the model jointly generates both solution rollouts and the task-specific harnesses that scaffold them, rather than relying on human-designed harnesses for RL. The 397B variant scores 77.5 on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and 82.4 on SWE-Bench Verified, edging out Claude Opus 4.7 on both.

title: The end of the AArch64 desktop experiment
blurb: After eleven months running an Ampere Altra system as his daily desktop, the author ended the experiment, citing an Altra PCIe erratum (PCIE_65) that corrupts MMIO writes to AMD GPUs and forced a self-built kernel rebuilt on every package update. His conclusion is that AArch64 on the desktop still fails on hardware meant for servers, where short QVLs and GPU incompatibilities turn ordinary use into ongoing maintenance.

title: I added dark mode by editing CSS variables not 100 components
blurb: The author shipped dark mode and per-user accent theming across a 130-component app without touching markup, by collapsing ~1,230 hardcoded indigo classes into a single semantic brand token defined as CSS variables. The key insight is that Tailwind v4 emits its palette as real CSS variables, so redefining the slate ramp inside a .dark selector remaps every text and background utility without editing a single component.

title: Decades-Old Bash Tricks Expose AI Coding Agents to Supply Chain Attacks
blurb: Adversa AI documented GuardFall, a class of bypasses where decades-old Bash behaviors like quote removal and $IFS spacing unwind a command's obfuscation after a coding agent's pattern-based shell guard has already approved it. Ten of eleven tested open source agents, including Hermes, OpenCode, and Roo-code, leave at least one gap open, letting a poisoned README or Makefile trigger silent credential exfiltration in CI pipelines running auto-yes.

title: N.I.H. Announces World’s Largest Integrated Health Database
blurb: The NIH launched what it calls the world's largest integrated health database, pairing more than 500,000 people's full genomes with their clinical records and wearable-device data. The combination lets researchers run genotype-to-phenotype studies at population scale, linking genetic variants to real-world disease progression and treatment response in ways siloed datasets cannot.

title: MongoDB embeds reranking into Atlas as enterprises look to simplify AI stacks for scale
blurb: MongoDB added Native Reranking to Atlas, a Voyage AI-powered feature now in public preview that runs inside the aggregation pipeline and improves retrieval quality by up to 30%. Folding reranking into the database removes a separate vendor, API, and orchestration layer from RAG stacks, cutting the retry logic and version juggling that a standalone reranking service forces on teams as apps scale.

title: Zilliz has a Loonatic storage engine
blurb: Zilliz released Loon, a storage engine for Zilliz Cloud that serves real-time vector search, large-scale discovery, and batch analytics from a single copy of data on object storage, using Vortex for vectors and Parquet for scalar fields under a shared versioned manifest. Zilliz's internal tests show Loon's layout pulls roughly 135x less data per record read than Parquet, and adding a new embedding model becomes a column-level version update rather than a multi-hundred-gigabyte rewrite.

title: Meister Varma Architects uses salvaged stone for home in India
blurb: Meister Varma Architects completed Shilaya, a home in Tamil Nadu's Nilgiri Mountains built from stone salvaged out of a colonial-era cottage ruin on the same site, paired with a prefabricated lightweight steel roof trucked in from the plains. The project is a working case study in material reuse under hard constraints, since local extraction is banned in the ecologically sensitive region, with half-metre twin-leaf stone walls supplying thermal mass against the area's cold winters.

title: Attackers Exploit SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558 to Deploy TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer
blurb: A maximum-severity authentication bypass in SimpleHelp's OIDC flow (CVE-2026-48558, CVSS 10.0) is being exploited to forge "Technician" sessions on internet-facing RMM servers, granting unauthenticated attackers privileged remote access that bypasses MFA via self-registration. Blackpoint Cyber observed the access used to deploy TaskWeaver, an obfuscated Node.js loader, and Djinn Stealer, which harvests credentials from cloud platforms, source control, SSH, AI dev tools, and crypto wallets across Windows, macOS, and Linux.

title: Decades-Old Bash Tricks Expose AI Coding Agents to Supply Chain Attacks
blurb: Adversa AI disclosed GuardFall, a structural flaw in which decades-old Bash parsing tricks like quote removal and $IFS spacing unwind obfuscated commands after pattern-based shell guards have already approved them, letting poisoned READMEs or Makefiles trigger silent execution in AI coding agents. Ten of eleven tested open-source agents, including Hermes, OpenCode, and Roo-code, left the gap open, exposing developer-authority credentials and CI pipelines running auto-yes modes to supply-chain compromise.

title: Insurance giant Aflac discloses data breach after subsidiary hack
blurb: Aflac disclosed a breach of its wholly owned Aflac Japan subsidiary, where an unauthorized third party accessed systems between June 15 and June 25, 2026, stealing policy and coverage details, personal information, and bank account data. The incident is contained to Japan and did not touch U.S. systems; Aflac has notified the Japan Financial Services Agency and will alert affected individuals as the investigation continues.

title: SimpleHelp vulnerability exploited to deliver mighty Djinn Stealer (CVE-2026-48558)
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-48558, a recently patched OIDC authentication bypass in SimpleHelp RMM, to bypass authentication on internet-facing servers, hijack technician sessions, and mass-deploy the cross-platform Djinn Stealer across managed systems. Disclosed June 12 by Horizon3.ai, the flaw lets operators inherit a trusted support-session execution path; Djinn harvests credentials for cloud platforms, source control, package registries, SSH, AI dev tools, and crypto wallets, so patch and rotate exposed secrets immediately.

title: Supreme Court Rules Constitutional Privacy Protections Apply to Cellphone Users’ Location History
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that constitutional privacy protections extend to cellphone location history, holding that users don't forfeit Fourth Amendment expectations by opting into Google's location tracking. The decision, stemming from a geofence warrant used to identify a bank robber, sends the case back to a lower court and signals tighter judicial scrutiny of geofence and reverse-location warrants going forward.

title: The Realities of AI Video Surveillance
blurb: AI is shifting video surveillance from preset object searches to natural-language behavioral queries, letting analysts hunt footage with terms like "two men handing off a bag" or "a vehicle repainted within a day." A Financial Times piece, citing use in Israel/Iran and Russia, shows intelligence officers can now search for behavior rather than objects, removing the scale limits that once made mass video review impractical.

title: Aflac Japan Data Breach Impacts 4.38 Million
blurb: Aflac Life Insurance Japan disclosed that attackers exfiltrated personal data on roughly 4.38 million customers and agents from its policyholder portal, with intruders accessing systems repeatedly between June 15 and discovery on June 25. The stolen records include names, addresses, dates of birth, and insurance account details, plus premium transfer account information for about 230,000 people, though no credit card data was taken.

title: AirDrop and Quick Share Flaws Let Nearby Attackers Trigger Crashes and Bypass Checks
blurb: Researchers at CISPA disclosed six flaws in Apple AirDrop and Android Quick Share that let an attacker in wireless range, with no prior pairing or user prompt, crash the macOS/iOS sharingd service and bypass Samsung's session checks, with the AirDrop bugs also taking down AirPlay, Handoff, and Continuity. Apple has patched one bug with a CVE and Google fixed the Windows crash, while the remaining AirDrop and Samsung issues stay in coordinated disclosure with no exploitation reported.

title: European digital ID wallets rely on safety services of Google and Apple
blurb: European digital identity wallets being rolled out across EU member states rely on Google's Play Integrity API and Apple's Managed Device Attestation to verify that wallet apps run on untampered hardware. Critics argue this embeds private-sector remote attestation into public infrastructure and reinforces Google's Android ecosystem control, since the API treats unlicensed Android variants as security risks, a likely Digital Markets Act conflict that the open Hardware Attestation API would avoid.

title: Microsoft adds smarter bot protection to Teams meetings
blurb: Microsoft added a Teams admin policy that automatically detects third-party meeting bots, routes them to the lobby, and requires organizer approval before they can join, even where lobby bypass is enabled. Admins assign the "Manage external bots" control per user or group across Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS, with allow lists, full-block policies, and bot audit logs planned next.

title: StarkWare unveils Starknet post-quantum roadmap, calling it crypto’s ‘strongest’ to date
blurb: StarkWare published a three-phase roadmap to make Starknet quantum-resistant, claiming it will be crypto's strongest post-quantum design once shipped over the coming months. The plan migrates Starknet's STARK-based proving and account signatures to post-quantum primitives, positioning the L2 ahead of a standards push as NIST-finalized algorithms move toward broader adoption.

title: AI browsers like Perplexity Comet can be tricked into spilling your password through BioShocking exploit
blurb: LayerX disclosed BioShocking, a prompt-injection technique that reframes credential theft as a game objective to bypass AI browser guardrails, exfiltrating saved passwords, session cookies, and tokens. All six agents tested fell for it (ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity Comet, Fellou, Genspark, Sigma, and Anthropic's Claude Chrome extension); OpenAI patched Atlas, Anthropic's fix reportedly failed to hold, and Perplexity closed the report without acting.

title: Hacking a Reverse Osmosis Water Filter Through its Smart Faucet
blurb: A researcher reverse-engineered the four-pin serial link between Waterdrop's G3P600 reverse-osmosis unit and its smart faucet, decoding a 9,600-baud protocol that exposes filter health, water quality, pump status, air temperature, and faucet state. A custom PCB now bridges the system into Home Assistant via ESPHome, demonstrating that the unprotected plaintext link offers full local monitoring and control to anyone who taps the cable.

title: NIST Enrichment Reductions Impact CVE Coverage, Accuracy
blurb: NIST scaled back the number of CVEs it selects for in-depth enrichment, and researchers report mixed results in the coverage and accuracy of the National Vulnerability Database. The reduction leaves defenders who rely on NVD metadata for vulnerability prioritization to fill gaps with third-party enrichment sources or risk acting on incomplete records.

title: How Spain's LaLiga uses nationwide IP blocking to combat illegal sports streams, impacting an estimated 550K+ domains, putting it in conflict with Cloudflare (Bloomberg)
blurb: LaLiga, Spain's top soccer league, uses court-ordered nationwide IP blocking to disrupt illegal match streams, a tactic Bloomberg estimates has affected 550K+ domains. The broad blocks sweep up legitimate sites sharing Cloudflare infrastructure, putting LaLiga in direct conflict with the CDN provider over collateral takedowns of small businesses and government agencies.

title: Spain's War on Football Piracy Knocks Legitimate Sites Offline
blurb: LaLiga's anti-piracy IP-blocking campaign in Spain has knocked legitimate websites offline, hitting small businesses, government agencies, and services that share infrastructure with the targeted streaming sites. The Spanish football league obtained court orders to block IPs hosting illegal match streams, but the broad blocks caught CDNs like Cloudflare, taking down unrelated sites that operators say have no recourse to appeal.

title: Sensitive iPhone supplier details were part of last week's data leak at Tata Electronics
blurb: A cyberattack on India-based Tata Electronics exfiltrated more than 630GB of data, including sensitive iPhone 18 Pro manufacturing details such as component specs and supplier relationships for hundreds of parts. Documents from fellow Tata clients Tesla and TSMC also appeared in the dark-web dump; Apple says it is investigating and working with Tata on long-term security measures.

title: Three AirDrop vulnerabilities discovered, with Apple working on a full fix
blurb: Researchers disclosed three pre-authentication AirDrop denial-of-service flaws affecting iPhone and Mac, with similar bugs in Android's Quick Share. A proximity attacker within Wi-Fi range can send one malformed request to an unrecognized path, triggering a Swift fatalError that crashes AirDrop, AirPlay, Handoff, Universal Clipboard, and Continuity Camera; Apple has patched one issue with an assigned CVE and is fixing the other two.

title: Vorlon debuts Guardian to block risky AI agent actions before they complete
blurb: Vorlon launched Guardian, a real-time enforcement gateway that sits at the protocol layer between AI agents and connected systems, applying policy to block out-of-bounds actions, mask sensitive data in transit, or downgrade an agent to read-only before a transaction completes. It covers any app or data store exposing an API or MCP server, enforcing per-system rules so a PII-holding data lake runs stricter than a project management tool.

title: What the Numbers Say About FIFA 2026 Cyber Risk
blurb: Check Point Research reports that the fraud infrastructure targeting the FIFA World Cup 2026 was pre-built and staged months ahead, with over one-third of official partners lacking DMARC enforcement to block domain spoofing and fake sportsbook apps surging to roughly 60 times baseline across at least five coordinated developer accounts. Defenders in the event supply chain should enforce DMARC, verify payment changes out-of-band, and monitor partner domains and app stores for impersonation infrastructure.

title: Attackers Exploit SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558 to Deploy TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-48558, a maximum-severity (CVSS 10.0) authentication bypass in SimpleHelp's OIDC flow, to forge Technician sessions on the RMM platform and deploy two new malware families, TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer. Organizations running SimpleHelp with generic or Azure AD OIDC should patch immediately, since the flaw bypasses MFA and Djinn Stealer harvests credentials for cloud platforms, source control, package registries, SSH, and AI coding assistants.

title: Supreme Court Rules Constitutional Privacy Protections Apply to Cellphone Users’ Location History
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that constitutional privacy protections extend to cellphone location history, holding that users do not forfeit Fourth Amendment expectations simply by opting into services like Google's location tracking. The decision raises the legal exposure of geofence warrants and pressures enterprises that collect or retain location data to tighten retention and government-request policies, with the underlying case remanded for further review.

title: AirDrop and Quick Share Flaws Let Nearby Attackers Trigger Crashes and Bypass Checks
blurb: Researchers at the CISPA Helmholtz Center disclosed six flaws in Apple's AirDrop and the Quick Share features from Samsung and Google, including a zero-interaction bug that crashes the macOS and iOS sharingd service and takes AirPlay, Handoff, Universal Clipboard, and Continuity Camera down with it. Apple, Google, and Samsung are coordinating fixes, with Apple already patching one bug and Google paying a bounty and shipping a code fix, and no exploitation reported so far.

title: SimpleHelp vulnerability exploited to deliver mighty Djinn Stealer (CVE-2026-48558)
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-48558, a recently patched OIDC authentication bypass in SimpleHelp RMM, to bypass authentication on internet-facing servers and mass-deploy the new Djinn Stealer across managed systems via trusted technician sessions. Because SimpleHelp is widely used by MSPs and internal help desks, a single compromised server exposes credentials for AWS, Azure, Okta, Cloudflare, source control, and SSH across every downstream client, so patching and auditing exposed instances is urgent.

title: Insurance giant Aflac discloses data breach after subsidiary hack
blurb: Aflac disclosed in an SEC filing that attackers breached its wholly owned Japan subsidiary between June 15 and June 25, accessing policy details, personal data, and bank account information for an as-yet-undetermined number of policyholders. The company says the intrusion was contained to Aflac Japan and did not touch its U.S. systems, marking the second breach disclosure for the supplemental insurer in a year amid sustained attacks on the sector.

title: Aflac Japan Data Breach Impacts 4.38 Million
blurb: Aflac Life Insurance Japan disclosed that attackers exfiltrated personal data on roughly 4.38 million customers and agents from its policyholder portal, including names, addresses, dates of birth, and insurance account details, plus bank transfer information for about 230,000 people. The breach is contained to Aflac Japan systems and does not touch the US business, but at least five services remain disrupted with no restoration timeline as the investigation continues.

title: European digital ID wallets rely on safety services of Google and Apple
blurb: European member states are embedding Google's Play Integrity API and Apple's Managed Device Attestation into their digital ID wallets, building public identity infrastructure on top of two private gatekeepers' remote-attestation services. The dependency risks DMA violations and locks out non-Google Android builds, even though an open alternative, Android's Hardware Attestation API, delivers the same hardware checks without enforcing Play Store and Google-account requirements.

title: Microsoft unveils Memora to tackle AI agents’ memory problem
blurb: Microsoft Research released Memora, a memory system that decouples what an agent stores from how it retrieves it, organizing knowledge around abstractions and cue-based lookup rather than raw conversation history. Microsoft claims it cuts context token usage by up to 98% while matching or beating full-context accuracy, positioning it against fact-extraction tools like Mem0 and graph-based systems such as Zep and GraphRAG.

title: Taiwan raids Supermicro and two supply-chain partners in widening Nvidia smuggling probe — nine sites hit as six people summoned for questioning
blurb: Taiwan's Keelung District Prosecutors' Office raided Supermicro's local office, six homes, and three affiliated sites including distributor Albatron and data center operator Chief Telecom, widening its probe into Nvidia AI chip diversion to China. Because Taiwanese law doesn't criminalize unauthorized AI-chip exports, prosecutors are charging document forgery rather than smuggling, and Supermicro shares fell 8% as Taipei weighs new legislation to restrict all China chip sales.

title: Meta fights soaring hardware costs by reusing old DDR4 server memory in new DDR5-only servers — custom CXL 2.0 chip marries legacy DDR4-2400 with cutting-edge DDR5-6400
blurb: Meta built a custom CXL 2.0 ASIC called Vistara to attach decommissioned DDR4 memory to new DDR5-only AMD EPYC Turin servers, expanding each MemServer node to 1TB by pairing 768GB of local DDR5-6400 with 256GB of reclaimed DDR4-2400. The move sidesteps record DDR5 prices by tiering cold data onto cheaper salvaged memory, a hyperscaler playbook that pressures enterprises to weigh CXL memory expanders before paying spot rates for new DRAM.

title: Microsoft adds smarter bot protection to Teams meetings
blurb: Microsoft added a Teams admin policy that detects third-party meeting bots, routes them to the lobby, and requires organizer approval before they can join, with the control assignable to individual users or groups from the Teams Admin Center. The feature targets malicious apps and unapproved note-taking or transcription bots, and Microsoft plans to follow with allow lists, full external-bot blocking, and admin audit logs.

title: SAP Gives AI Product Oversight to Top Executives in Reshuffle
blurb: SAP distributed oversight of its product and engineering organization across its CEO and COO in its second top-level reorganization this year, as Europe's largest software company moves to keep pace with AI competition. The reshuffle pushes AI accountability into the executive suite, a signal to enterprise buyers weighing SAP's roadmap that the vendor is treating its AI product strategy as a board-level priority rather than a divisional one.

title: MongoDB embeds reranking into Atlas as enterprises look to simplify AI stacks for scale
blurb: MongoDB introduced Native Reranking for Atlas, a public-preview feature powered by Voyage AI that runs inside the aggregation pipeline and improves AI retrieval quality by up to 30% without a separate reranking service. For CIOs, the move collapses one more vendor, API, and orchestration layer into the existing database, cutting governance overhead and inference cost as RAG applications scale.

title: Apple may have to allow 3rd-party app payments and Apple Pay rivals in UK
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority proposed forcing Apple to let developers steer iPhone users to third-party payment options and to open the NFC chip to Apple Pay rivals, while barring Apple from charging the unreasonable "steering" fees it has used to blunt similar rules in the US and EU. Any permitted fees must be lower than current App Store commissions, with the savings passed to consumers or reinvested.

title: NIST Enrichment Reductions Impact CVE Coverage, Accuracy
blurb: NIST scaled back the number of CVEs it selects for in-depth enrichment, and researchers report the change has produced mixed effects on vulnerability coverage and data accuracy. Enterprise security teams that depend on NVD metadata for prioritization and patch workflows now face thinner analysis on lower-priority CVEs, pushing more triage burden onto internal staff or commercial vulnerability-intelligence vendors.

title: Apple is speeding up software patching due to AI security concerns – here’s what you need to know
blurb: Apple released an out-of-band patch, iOS 25.6.2, breaking from its usual practice of bundling security fixes into larger updates, and told Reuters the move reflects concern that AI is shortening the gap between a flaw's discovery and its exploitation. For IT teams, the faster patch cadence raises the operational tempo of mobile fleet management, with Google's GTIG already reporting AI-built zero-day exploits in the wild.

title: Digital Realty to buy Blackstone’s stake in three Virginia data centres
blurb: Digital Realty agreed to buy Blackstone's blended 64% equity stake in three fully leased Northern Virginia data centres for $3.5B, valuing the 288-megawatt portfolio at $7.8B and paying $1.2B in cash plus $2.3B in stock. The deal hands the REIT stabilized, hyperscaler-leased capacity in the most power-constrained data-centre market in the US, bypassing the multi-year permitting and grid-interconnection delays a ground-up build would carry.

title: How Companies Are Managing AI Token Spend
blurb: Enterprise AI token spend is climbing as companies deploy more AI agents, and IT teams are responding by applying FinOps practices from the cloud era to monitor and cap usage. Tactics include routing simpler queries to cheaper models, caching frequent responses, and setting per-team token budgets, treating tokens as a metered resource like compute the same way they learned to govern cloud bills.

title: Startup OpenMatter wants to make enterprises prove what their AI agents do
blurb: OpenMatter launched a "verifiable trust layer" that uses cryptography to prove how enterprise data is used and how AI agents behave across systems a company doesn't own, layering on top of existing cloud, data, and AI tools rather than replacing them. The platform combines confidential compute, agent policy enforcement, and a cryptographically provable execution log, targeting healthcare, financial analytics, and secure model training where sensitive data crosses organizational boundaries.

title: Dragonfly v2.5.0 is released
blurb: Dragonfly v2.5.0 adds direct repository downloads from Hugging Face and ModelScope, routing Git LFS model weights through its P2P network while a new Kubernetes mutating webhook injects P2P capability into Pods without rebuilding images. The release also ships comprehensive rate limiting across the control plane and client, a Manager-console blocklist for emergency download control, and a dfctl CLI for managing local task storage.

title: Meta fights soaring hardware costs by reusing old DDR4 server memory in new DDR5-only servers — custom CXL 2.0 chip marries legacy DDR4-2400 with cutting-edge DDR5-6400
blurb: Meta built Vistara, an in-house CXL 2.0 ASIC that attaches DDR4 RDIMMs recovered from decommissioned servers to new DDR5-only AMD Turin machines, dodging record DDR5 prices by reclaiming memory that would otherwise be scrapped. Each MemServer pairs 768GB of local DDR5-6400 with 256GB of CXL-attached DDR4-2400 exposed as a separate NUMA node, letting Linux migrate cold pages to the slower 76GB/s tier while hot data stays in 614GB/s DDR5.

title: Kepler, re-architected: Improved power accuracy and a community call to action!
blurb: Kepler, the CNCF sandbox project that attributes Kubernetes power consumption per Pod and exports it as Prometheus metrics, has been rewritten to drop its eBPF dependency, which required CAP_BPF and CAP_SYSADMIN privileges and under-reported energy from short-lived processes. The new architecture aims to improve power-attribution accuracy, ease deployment on VMs, and lower the barrier for community contributions ahead of training power-estimation models.

title: Hypefy AI raises $7.2M Series A to automate global influencer campaigns
blurb: Hypefy AI raised $7.2M in a Series A led by AYMO Ventures to automate end-to-end influencer campaign execution, from creator discovery and outreach to pricing, contracting, content review, and payments. The Croatia-based platform has run thousands of campaigns across 43 countries for brands including Unilever, PepsiCo, and Samsung, with marketers keeping control of briefs, budgets, and approvals while AI handles the operational work.

title: How to track your brand’s presence in AI search
blurb: Ranking first in traditional search no longer guarantees AI visibility: a Semrush analysis of 200,000 Google AI Overviews found the top organic result was cited only 34% of the time on mobile and 46% on desktop. Tracking presence in answer engines like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Perplexity now means monitoring a fixed set of prompts and logging three new metrics: mentions, owned citations, and share of voice against competitors. SEO still feeds the model, but the unit of measurement has shifted from a ranked list of blue links to whether your brand makes it into the synthesized answer.

title: Instagram Tests New Ways to Surface ‘Your Algorithm’ Controls
blurb: Instagram is testing more prominent access points for its "Your Algorithm" controls, including pull-down feed menus, swipe-up Reel prompts, and per-Reel more/less buttons, as Adam Mosseri pushes to make the feature central rather than buried in settings. With Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube all moving to explicit interest modeling, the competitive edge for brands shifts toward audience ownership and off-platform diversification rather than gaming distribution.

title: “Made with AI” gets lower engagement
blurb: Labeling social posts as AI-generated lowers engagement, according to USC research spanning 8 experiments and 1M TikTok posts. Posts marked "made with AI" drew 7-8% fewer likes and 7% lower combined comments and shares at equal quality, with viewers rating creators as putting in 15.6% less effort and feeling 14.5% less connected to them. If you use AI, show the human work behind it and explain why, rather than letting the label stand alone.

title: Instagram Tests New Ways to Surface ‘Your Algorithm’ Controls
blurb: Instagram is testing new entry points for "Your Algorithm," its feed-customization tool, including pull-down menus and per-Reel buttons that let users dial topics up or down without digging into settings. Head Adam Mosseri framed the goal as making interest controls central to the app, part of a broader industry shift to explicit interest modeling that pushes creators and brands to compete on monetization and audience ownership rather than distribution.

title: Booking.com still runs on the internal A/B testing framework its engineers wrote in Amsterdam in the mid-2000s, with no major rewrite since, and at any given moment it is running more than 1,000 live experiments on the same booking page you’re looking at
blurb: Booking.com still routes its entire site through an in-house experimentation platform its Amsterdam team built in the mid-2000s and never replaced, running more than 1,000 concurrent A/B tests on live production pages at any moment. The takeaway for PMs is that durable experimentation infrastructure, not a vendor tool, is what lets hundreds of squads ship overlapping tests against the same conversion funnel without contaminating each other's results.

title: 10x Coding Speed Won't 10x Revenue
blurb: The author argues that 10x faster coding won't translate into 10x revenue, because product success has always been gated by the market, not engineering speed. If teams ship 100 products where they used to ship 10, the same ~4 will win, dropping the success rate from 4-in-10 to roughly 4-in-100 since software budgets and the broader economy aren't growing 10x to match. The takeaway for PMs: cheap code shifts the real bottleneck to go-to-market, so prioritize finding real buyers with real pain over raw output.

title: How top PMs increase their leverage with AI
blurb: Lenny argues the PM role is shifting away from coordinating and aligning people toward hands-on building, with the strongest PMs now prototyping in real code, querying data conversationally through MCP, and running coding agents directly. The piece doubles as a pitch for a new "AI-Native Builder" course, but the underlying claim is the takeaway for PMs: leverage increasingly comes from doing the technical work yourself rather than handing it off, using tools like Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor inside discovery and shipping.

title: New York Life makes tokenization debut with onchain high-yield bond fund with Centrifuge
blurb: New York Life Investment Management, the $807 billion asset management arm of the insurer, launched its first tokenized fund through Centrifuge, bringing a US high-yield corporate bond strategy onchain with subscriptions and redemptions settled in USDC. The launch pushes Wall Street tokenization past Treasuries and private credit into higher-yield fixed income, adding NYLIM to a Centrifuge roster that already includes Apollo and Janus Henderson.

title: Nasdaq expands distribution of its market data into blockchain infrastructure
blurb: Nasdaq will distribute its TotalView full depth-of-book equity market data through the Pyth Network, publishing one of its core data products on the Pyth Data Marketplace as a programmable feed rather than a traditional terminal product. The move adds Nasdaq to a roster of on-chain data publishers including Tradeweb, SGX, and the US Department of Commerce, extending equity market data to tokenized-asset and on-chain settlement applications.

title: UK to lower stablecoin capital buffers, undercutting EU's MiCA requirements
blurb: The UK's Financial Conduct Authority proposed cutting the capital buffer for stablecoin issuers to 1% of circulating value, half the 2% equivalent under the EU's MiCA regime. The move follows the Bank of England abandoning its planned £20,000 ($26,500) per-person holding cap, signaling a lighter-touch UK framework aimed at attracting larger issuers as global stablecoin rules take shape.

title: New York Life makes first tokenized move partnering with Centrifuge on high-yield corporate bond strategy
blurb: New York Life Investment Management partnered with Centrifuge to tokenize its institutional high-yield corporate bond strategy, marking the insurer's first move into on-chain fund infrastructure. The tokenized fund opens NYLIM's established credit strategy to eligible investors via Centrifuge's RWA platform, extending the wave of traditional asset managers bringing fixed-income products on-chain.

title: StarkWare unveils Starknet post-quantum roadmap, calling it crypto’s ‘strongest’ to date
blurb: StarkWare published a three-phase roadmap to make Starknet quantum-resistant, replacing its current STARK proving and signature stack with post-quantum cryptography over the coming months. The plan covers quantum-safe account signatures, proof systems, and a migration path for existing assets, positioning Starknet ahead of most L2s on a threat that becomes material once large-scale quantum computers can break elliptic-curve keys.

title: Bitcoin $4.4 billion supply overhang emerges as institutional demand wilts
blurb: Bitcoin ETFs redeemed a record 71,600 BTC ($4 billion) in June while corporate treasuries bought just 7,500 BTC, leaving a net supply overhang near 77,000 BTC ($4.4 billion) once daily mined coins are counted. The imbalance, flagged by Glassnode, undercuts any recovery for BTC stuck near $60,000, with Strategy adding pressure by authorizing up to $1.25 billion in potential bitcoin sales.

title: Bitcoin nears 2024 lows as options traders pay up for downside protection
blurb: Bitcoin fell 1.5% on Tuesday to around $59,250, near its lowest level since late 2024, while ether tested multiyear support at $1,580 after twice bouncing from that level. BTC puts traded at a double-digit premium to calls despite subdued volatility indexes, signaling demand for downside protection as a failure to hold support would leave both tokens without an obvious floor.

title: UK to lower stablecoin capital buffers, undercutting EU's MiCA requirements
blurb: The FCA cut its proposed capital requirement for stablecoin issuers to 1% of circulating value from 2%, undercutting the 2% buffer mandated under the EU's MiCA regime. The move, which follows the Bank of England abandoning its £20,000 individual stablecoin holding cap, positions the UK as a lighter-touch jurisdiction for issuers weighing where to anchor their European operations.

title: New York Life makes tokenization debut with onchain high-yield bond fund with Centrifuge
blurb: New York Life Investment Management, the $807B asset-management arm of the insurer, launched its first tokenized fund, partnering with Centrifuge to bring its U.S. High Yield Corporate Bond Strategy onchain with subscriptions and redemptions settled in USDC. The move pushes Wall Street tokenization beyond Treasuries and private credit into higher-yield fixed income, joining BlackRock, Franklin Templeton, and Apollo on Centrifuge's platform.

title: New York Life makes first tokenized move partnering with Centrifuge on high-yield corporate bond strategy
blurb: New York Life Investment Management partnered with Centrifuge to tokenize its institutional high-yield corporate bond strategy, the insurer's first move into onchain assets. The fund gives eligible investors onchain access to a previously institutions-only credit strategy, signaling that traditional asset managers increasingly view tokenization as a distribution channel rather than an experiment.

title: BOE’s Breeden Says AI Agents Could Amplify Market Stress
blurb: Bank of England Deputy Governor Sarah Breeden warned that autonomous AI agents could amplify market volatility during periods of stress and may require tighter regulation, speaking at the ECB's annual symposium in Sintra. The comments signal that central banks are beginning to treat agentic trading and decision-making as a systemic risk, putting financial-stability oversight of AI on the regulatory agenda.

title: Nasdaq expands distribution of its market data into blockchain infrastructure
blurb: Nasdaq will distribute its TotalView full depth-of-book equity data through the Pyth Network, publishing one of its core market-data products on the Pyth Data Marketplace via a programmable interface rather than traditional terminals and dedicated feeds. The move puts a major exchange operator's data directly onto blockchain rails, alongside Pyth contributors like Tradeweb, SGX and the U.S. Department of Commerce, as Wall Street builds infrastructure for tokenized assets and onchain settlement.

title: How the Supreme Court Ushered in Corporate Chaos in D.C.
blurb: The Supreme Court affirmed the Federal Reserve's political independence while granting the presidency expanded authority to remove leaders at dozens of other federal regulators. The ruling unsettles the supervisory regimes that fintechs and banks depend on, since agencies like the OCC, FDIC, CFPB, and FTC could now shift enforcement posture with each administration rather than operating at arm's length from the White House.

title: Apple may have to allow 3rd-party app payments and Apple Pay rivals in UK
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority proposed forcing Apple to let developers steer iPhone users to third-party payment options outside the App Store, while barring the "malicious compliance" fees Apple has used in the US and EU to neutralize similar rulings. The CMA would also require Apple to open the iPhone's NFC chip to Apple Pay rivals, letting banks and fintechs run contactless wallets that have been locked out of the platform.

title: AI Leverage Is More Worrying Than Valuations, IMF’s Adrian Says
blurb: The IMF's Tobias Adrian said the rapid growth of AI-related debt issuance poses a greater financial-stability risk than stretched equity valuations, as data-center buildouts increasingly rely on leverage rather than cash. The warning shifts regulatory attention toward credit exposure across banks and private-credit funds underwriting AI infrastructure, a channel where losses could spread faster than an equity correction.
